era_context: The Seleucid Empire under Seleucus II Callinicus is experiencing significant internal and external pressures. The Han Dynasty in China, under Emperor Wen, is consolidating power following the Rebellion of the Seven States (154–151 BCE). The Hellenistic world is in a period of relative stability before the rise of Rome as a dominant Mediterranean power.

events.title: Aftermath of the Rebellion of the Seven States in Han China
events.description: The Rebellion of the Seven States (154–151 BCE) has recently concluded, and Emperor Wen of Han is consolidating centralized authority. The rebellion, led by regional kings who feared loss of power, has been suppressed, and Wen implements reforms to prevent future challenges to central authority. These reforms include reducing the power of regional kingdoms, strengthening the central bureaucracy, and implementing economic policies that promote stability and recovery. The successful suppression of this rebellion marks a turning point in Han Dynasty history, establishing the precedent for centralized imperial authority.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: The Rebellion of the Seven States (154–151 BCE) is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 187 BCE falls within the consolidation period following the rebellion, which is secure in the historical record.
cross_references: Follows from -154-001 (Rebellion of the Seven States)
Leads to -141-001 (Emperor Wu's accession and expansion)

events.title: Seleucus II Callinicus faces internal fragmentation
events.description: Seleucus II Callinicus, ruling the Seleucid Empire, faces significant challenges to his authority from regional governors and rival claimants. The empire is fragmenting as eastern satrapies fall under Parthian control and western territories are threatened by Galatian tribes and other regional powers. Seleucus II's reign is marked by military campaigns to reassert control, but these efforts are only partially successful. The internal fragmentation of the Seleucid Empire during this period sets the stage for its eventual decline and the rise of Rome as the dominant Mediterranean power.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Seleucid fragmentation during Seleucus II's reign (246–226 BCE) is well-attested in later sources. Specific events within 187 BCE are not individually dated in surviving texts.

events.title: Ptolemy III Euergetes I maintains Ptolemaic power
events.description: Ptolemy III Euergetes I continues his reign over Egypt, maintaining Ptolemaic naval and economic dominance in the eastern Mediterranean. His reign is characterized by cultural patronage, including support for the Library of Alexandria and Greek scholarship. Ptolemaic Egypt remains one of the wealthiest and most stable powers in the Hellenistic world, in contrast to the fragmenting Seleucid Empire.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy III's reign (246–222 BCE) is well-documented through Egyptian papyri and Greek sources. The general pattern of Ptolemaic stability during his reign is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and significance of specific Seleucid military campaigns under Seleucus II are disputed by modern scholars. Some sources suggest that the empire's fragmentation was more gradual than dramatic, with Parthian expansion occurring over decades rather than in sudden conquests. The extent to which Seleucus II's campaigns were successful or merely delaying actions is debated. Additionally, some scholars question whether the 'Rebellion of the Seven States' in China was as unified or as significant as traditional accounts suggest, though the general fact of the rebellion and its suppression is secure.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 187 BCE is moderate but geographically and chronologically uneven. Chinese sources (Shiji, Han Shu) provide detailed, near-contemporary records of Han Dynasty events, with the Rebellion of the Seven States being particularly well-documented. Hellenistic history relies heavily on later Greek and Roman compilations (Polybius, Appian, Strabo) written centuries after events. Polybius is generally considered more reliable for this period than Appian. Egyptian papyri provide administrative detail but limited narrative of major events. The lack of contemporary Seleucid sources is a significant gap in understanding the empire's internal dynamics. Western sources are biased toward Mediterranean events and may underrepresent or misrepresent events in Asia.

graph_edges.note: Han consolidation under Wen enables Emperor Wu's expansionist policies
Seleucid fragmentation accelerates, eventually establishing Parthia as a major power
Han consolidation occurs while Seleucid Empire fragments
